common_name: Fiddle-Leaf Philodendron (Horsehead Philodendron, Panda Plant, Panduraeforme)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio vine in zone 9; Philodendron bipennifolium cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 60°F. A moss pole or climbing support is beneficial — the leaves grow progressively larger as the plant climbs. Grow in a well-draining, aroid potting mix. Allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. Propagate from single-node stem cuttings in water or moist media. The leaf shape varies greatly between juvenile and mature forms. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Fiddle-Leaf Philodendron produces its most dramatic and largest leaves with consistent bright indirect light and a climbing support, but adapts to lower light conditions without serious decline. Philodendron bipennifolium (formerly classified as P. panduraeforme, a name still commonly used in horticultural trade) is a climbing philodendron native to Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru in the family Araceae. The common names 'Fiddle-Leaf Philodendron' and 'Horsehead Philodendron' both refer to the distinctive shape of the adult leaves — a broad, slightly elongated outline with two distinctive basal lobes and a characteristic narrowing in the middle that creates a violin or fiddle silhouette, or, from another angle, a horse's head profile. The leaves are glossy, bright to dark-green, leathery, and held on long petioles that give the plant an open, airy structure. In the juvenile form, the leaves are much smaller and simpler; the characteristic fiddle shape develops progressively as the plant matures. As a rapidly growing, adaptable climbing philodendron with an unusual and attractive leaf form, P. bipennifolium has long been popular in cultivation and has been used in breeding the modern 'Philodendron Florida' hybrid series. All parts contain calcium oxalate and are toxic if ingested.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Philodendron bipennifolium species notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron species for indoor culture
American Horticulture Society — Fiddle-leaf Philodendron cultivation guide
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Philodendron (toxic to pets)

common_name: Cannonball Tree (Nagalingam, Sala Tree, Shivalinga Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained, fertile, moist soil; Cannonball Tree is frost-sensitive and suitable only for zone 10-11 outdoor planting. In zone 9, it can be grown as a large container specimen. Cauliflorous (trunk-flowering) trees like this one should never be heavily pruned — the permanent framework of the trunk and branches is the bearing surface for all flowers and fruits. Site where the striking trunk flowers and falling fruits can be appreciated — do not plant where falling cannonball fruits (6–12 inch diameter, 5–10 lbs) could fall on people or vehicles.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil — Cannonball Tree grows most rapidly and blooms most prolifically in full sun with regular watering, becoming a dramatic, multi-season ornamental after 5–10 years. Couroupita guianensis is a large tropical tree native to tropical South America (Guiana, Venezuela, Peru, Brazil) in the Brazil-nut family (Lecythidaceae), famous for two simultaneous ornamental spectacles occurring directly on its trunk and branches. The flowers are produced year-round on long, pendant cauliflorous racemes (flower stalks) that hang directly from the trunk and major branches — each fragrant, complex, waxy flower 3–4 inches across with large, creamy-white to pale pink petals surrounding a modified stamen structure in pink, orange, and yellow that creates an exotic, orchid-like appearance. Simultaneously, the tree bears the previous season's fruits: enormous, perfectly spherical, hard-shelled, rust-brown fruits 6–12 inches in diameter (the 'cannonballs') that are attached in clusters to the same trunk racemes as the flowers — so the tree often displays flowers and fruits simultaneously. The fruits fall explosively when ripe, with a loud impact that explains the 'Cannonball Tree' name. In Hindu and Buddhist temple gardens throughout South and Southeast Asia (where the tree is sacred as the 'Shivalinga Tree' and 'Sala Tree'), the trunk-borne flowers and spherical fruits have profound religious symbolism.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Couroupita guianensis cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Couroupita guianensis for South Florida
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Couroupita guianensis species profile
Smithsonian Tropical Research Institute — Cauliflory in Couroupita guianensis

common_name: Pride of Burma (Orchid Tree of Burma, Queen of Flowering Trees)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in partial shade with consistently moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Pride of Burma is strictly a zone 10-11 tree and requires warm, humid, protected conditions to thrive. It performs best in partial shade and protection from strong winds, which damage its unusually large, pendant new leaves. The new foliage emerges in a remarkable coppery-red to orange-pink pendulous cascade before stiffening and turning green. This species is considered among the most demanding of tropical ornamental trees to cultivate well outside its native habitat.

directions: Plant in partial shade in consistently moist, fertile, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; Pride of Burma is one of the most demanding of all ornamental trees and requires the warm, humid, protected conditions of its native Myanmar habitat — consistently high humidity, warm temperatures year-round, shelter from drying winds, and fertile moist soil. Amherstia nobilis is an evergreen tree from the tropical forests of Myanmar (Burma), monotypic in its genus (the only species of Amherstia), and considered by many botanists and horticulturists to be the single most beautiful flowering tree in the world — a claim contested only by a handful of other extraordinary trees. The tree produces its flowers in pendant racemes hanging from the branch tips: each raceme carries 15–30 individual flowers of extraordinary complexity — vivid scarlet-red sepals, yellow-tipped petals, scarlet wings, arranged in a structure resembling an ornate tropical orchid flower in brilliant red and yellow. Each flower is 3–4 inches long; a single raceme in full development is among the most spectacular botanical displays in existence. The new leaves that emerge before the flowers are equally remarkable: they hang in long, copper-red to orange-pink, perfectly limp pendant tassels up to 3 feet long before gradually stiffening and turning green as they mature. Named in honor of Lady Amherst, wife of the Governor-General of India, who first preserved specimens for Western science in 1826.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Amherstia nobilis species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Amherstia nobilis cultivation notes
Missouri Botanical Garden — Amherstia nobilis: Pride of Burma
Singapore Botanic Gardens — Amherstia nobilis cultural requirements

common_name: Indian Almond (Tropical Almond, Beach Almond, Sea Almond)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Terminalia catappa is extremely salt-tolerant and thrives in sandy coastal soils. It is frost-sensitive in zone 9 but recovers rapidly from cold damage. The tree is briefly deciduous in late fall — leaves turn brilliant red, orange, and yellow before falling, a striking display unusual in tropical trees. The leaves and bark are widely used in aquaculture for their tannins and antimicrobial properties; Indian almond leaves are added to aquariums to lower pH and release beneficial compounds. The edible nuts are encased in a fibrous husk that requires processing.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile soil — Indian Almond is extremely drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established, making it one of the best trees for exposed coastal sites in zone 10-11. Terminalia catappa is a large, deciduous to briefly deciduous tropical tree of uncertain original native range (possibly the Malay Archipelago and Indian Ocean Islands) that has been transported and naturalized throughout tropical coastlines worldwide by human action over millennia, now found on virtually every tropical coast from South and Southeast Asia through the Pacific Islands, tropical Africa, the Caribbean, Central America, and South Florida. The tree has a distinctive tiered, horizontal branching pattern that creates a naturally formal, layered silhouette. Large, glossy, oval leaves (8–12 inches) turn spectacular shades of red, orange, and crimson before dropping twice per year (briefly deciduous in dry or cool periods), a display of fall color unusual in the tropics. The fruits are oval drupes (1.5–2 inches) containing a hard inner kernel that, when extracted and dried, has an almond-like flavor and is eaten throughout Asia and the Pacific — though the extraction requires significant processing. The large leaves are prized in aquarium and aquaculture for their tannins, which lower pH, release antimicrobial compounds, and replicate conditions of tropical black-water streams favored by certain fish.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Terminalia catappa for Florida landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Terminalia catappa cultivation
USDA Forest Service — Terminalia catappa species profile
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Trees for Gulf Coast Zone 10 Landscapes

common_name: Malay Apple (Mountain Apple, Jambu Bol, Rose Apple Tree)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun to partial shade with consistently moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Malay Apple is frost-sensitive and suitable for zone 10-11 outdoor planting or zone 9 container culture. The tree exhibits cauliflory — flowers and fruits emerge directly from the trunk and major branches, not the growing tips. Fruiting begins within 3–5 years of planting. The fruits mature in spring to early summer and must be harvested promptly as they do not store well.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Malay Apple grows most rapidly with regular watering and fertilization in the warm humid conditions of zone 10-11 Gulf Coast and South Florida. Syzygium malaccense is a large, fast-growing, evergreen fruit tree native to Malaysia and the Indonesian islands, now cultivated throughout the tropics from the Pacific Islands to Central America, Africa, and the Caribbean. The tree is doubly ornamental: in spring, it produces spectacular displays of crimson-red to deep rose-purple flowers that emerge directly from the bark of the trunk and major branches (cauliflory), creating an extraordinary effect of flowers growing from the wood — dozens to hundreds of 1-inch flowers with brush-like clusters of bright crimson stamens covering the trunk in a fiery display. These flowers are followed by the fruits: dark red to magenta, pear-shaped, 2–3 inch fruits with white, spongy, mildly sweet flesh and a faint floral flavor — eaten fresh throughout Southeast Asia and the Pacific Islands and used in jams and beverages. The tree is an important crop and dooryard fruit tree throughout tropical Oceania and Southeast Asia. The Hawaiian name 'Ohia Ai' (mountain apple) reflects its long cultivation history in the Hawaiian Islands. In zone 10-11 South Florida landscapes, it serves as both an ornamental shade tree and a productive home fruit tree.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Syzygium malaccense (Malay Apple)
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Syzygium malaccense cultivation
Hawaii Cooperative Extension Service — Malay Apple cultivation in Hawaii
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Syzygium malaccense
